title: [Feature]: Publish a hardened Docker image
problem: Users can install the package with Python tooling and run systemd services, but container-based environments need a maintained Docker image. A container image must be designed carefully so it does not run as root unnecessarily, does not bake in secrets, and can support optional sink extras without becoming too broad.
proposal: Add a hardened container image build for the nats-sink CLI with clear variants or build arguments for optional extras. The image should run as a non-root user, use a minimal base, include health-check guidance, support mounted JSON configuration and secret injection, and publish immutable release artifacts through the release workflow when appropriate.
users: Operators deploying nats-sinks in container platforms, maintainers validating release artifacts, and security teams reviewing runtime isolation.
delivery_semantics: Containerization must not change commit-then-ACK behavior. Shutdown handling must preserve the existing graceful stop model, and container health checks must reflect readiness without causing message loss.
security: The image must not contain credentials, local wallets, certificate material, test configs, or build secrets. It should use a non-root user, avoid unnecessary packages, pin dependencies through the release artifact, and document filesystem write locations.
acceptance: A Dockerfile or image build definition is added with security comments.
The image runs nats-sink with mounted configuration.
Release automation can build and optionally publish image artifacts.
Container documentation includes least-privilege and secret handling guidance.
Image build checks are included in CI or release validation where practical.
tests: Add smoke tests for image build and CLI help where container tooling is available, plus documentation and security checks. Run scripts/check.sh.
documentation: Update docs/operations.md, docs/development.md, docs/security.md, docs/release.md, README.md, ROADMAP.md, and CHANGELOG.md.
closeout: Close after a release provides the container build path. Include release tag, image variant details, checks run, and any publishing limitations.
